common_name: Florida Anise Shrub (Spicy-Scented Native Shade Shrub with Dark Red Spring Flowers)

planting.note: Plant container plants in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, strongly acidic (pH 4.5–6.0) soil in partial to deep shade; Florida Anise Shrub is not related to culinary anise and is toxic — the pleasant anise-like fragrance from the crushed leaves comes from anethole compounds, but the plant contains toxic substances and should never be used in cooking or herbal preparations.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, strongly acidic (pH 4.5–6.0) soil in partial to deep shade; Florida Anise Shrub (Illicium floridanum) is a native Gulf South evergreen shrub of extraordinary shade tolerance and distinctive character — tolerating deep shade under magnolias and live oaks while producing one of the most unusual spring flower displays of any native shrub: dark red, multi-petaled, starry flowers with narrow, strap-shaped petals arranged in a loose, spidery pattern, appearing in April–May with a pleasant but faint sweet-spicy fragrance. The large, glossy, dark-green leaves are the plant's dominant feature through most of the year — broader and more tropical-looking than most native shrubs — and when crushed or bruised they release a strong, pleasant anise-spice fragrance from volatile oils, giving the plant its common name. The fragrance is from anethole compounds similar to those in culinary anise, but Florida anise shrub is toxic and must never be confused with star anise or used in food preparation. Florida Anise Shrub grows naturally in moist, shaded ravines and creek banks in the Florida panhandle and along the Gulf Coastal plain from Florida to Louisiana — it is the quintessential native Gulf South shade shrub, evolved under the same dense-canopy conditions that dominate Gulf South residential landscapes. The fast growth rate allows it to reach privacy-screen heights quickly, and the dense, branching evergreen habit creates an impenetrable visual screen. An outstanding native hedge shrub for shaded property edges. All parts are toxic if ingested.

common_name: Japanese Aralia (Bold Tropical-Looking Deep-Shade Evergreen Shrub)

planting.note: Plant container plants in spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to deep shade; Fatsia japonica is one of the few large shrubs that performs well in deep shade — it is an outstanding choice for north-facing exposures, under building overhangs, and under large trees where other shrubs fail to thrive.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to deep shade; Japanese Aralia (Fatsia japonica) is one of the most bold-textured, tropical-looking evergreen shrubs available for deep-shade garden positions in the Gulf South — a native of coastal Japan and Korea that produces very large (12–18 inch across), deeply lobed, palmate, glossy dark-green leaves on long petioles, creating an extraordinarily lush and dramatic visual effect in shaded garden spaces. The leaves are genuinely large — among the biggest of any hardy evergreen shrub commonly grown in the Gulf South — and their tropical character is so convincing that Fatsia can appear exotic and out-of-place in naturalistic settings but is perfectly at home in bold subtropical shade designs. In November–December, Fatsia produces large clusters of small, creamy-white flowers in rounded umbels arranged in compound panicles — a winter bloom when virtually no other shade shrubs are flowering — followed by small black berries that provide winter bird food. The winter flowering is unexpected and welcomed in the shade garden during the season's low point. Fatsia grows relatively quickly into a multi-stemmed shrub that spreads by suckers and can form substantial clumps. The plant is remarkably tolerant of deep shade, dry soil (once established), coastal salt spray, urban air pollution, and root competition — a combination of tolerances that makes it one of the most versatile and reliable large evergreen shrubs for difficult Gulf South shade positions. Pruning to reduce height or remove suckers is best done in late winter or early spring.

common_name: Japanese Aucuba (Glossy Deep-Shade Evergreen Shrub with Red Winter Berries)

planting.note: Plant container plants in spring in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to deep shade; Aucuba is dioecious — plants are either male or female, and both are needed for the female plant to produce its ornamental red berries; plant at least one male within 30–40 feet of female plants.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to deep shade; Japanese Aucuba (Aucuba japonica) is one of the most reliable and widely planted deep-shade evergreen shrubs for Gulf South landscapes, valued for its ability to maintain attractive, glossy, large-leafed evergreen presence in positions of deep, dry shade under large trees where most other shrubs fail completely or perform poorly. The large (4–8 inch), glossy, dark green leaves are the primary ornamental feature in the all-green form — clean, lustrous, and bold-textured throughout the year. Female plants produce clusters of bright scarlet to red berries from December through spring, providing significant winter ornamental interest and bird food; the berry display requires a male pollinator plant nearby. The gold-dust variegated form (A. japonica 'Variegata') with its yellow-spotted leaves is the most frequently grown and most ornamentally dramatic selection — the bold yellow spots on glossy dark-green leaves create a luminous effect in deep shade that no other plant quite matches. Aucuba is exceptionally shade-tolerant, performing in the deep dry shade under large trees where even cast iron plant (Aspidistra) and other ultra-tough shade plants grow poorly. The tolerance for drought, dense shade, and root competition gives Aucuba a practical advantage in Gulf South landscapes that few other evergreen shrubs can match. Avoid direct sun and wet, poorly drained soil — both cause leaf scorch and root rot respectively. An excellent choice for north and east exposures under building eaves.

common_name: Blue Hawaii Taro (Blue-Green Foliage Purple-Stemmed Variegated Elephant Ear)

planting.note: Plant tubers or container plants after the last frost in moist to wet, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; Blue Hawaii requires consistent moisture and performs well in pond margins, bog gardens, and low areas with consistent water — drought causes leaf stress and color loss significantly faster than in standard green-leaved colocasia.

directions: Plant in moist to wet, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; Blue Hawaii is a distinctive Colocasia esculenta cultivar producing medium-to-large, heart-shaped leaves in a rich blue-green to teal-green color with prominent darker veining and a contrasting deep purple-black stem coloration — the combination of blue-green leaf blades with dark purple stems creating a striking two-toned visual effect unlike the plain green of standard taro or the pure black of 'Black Magic'. The blue-green leaf color is unusual among elephant ear cultivars and gives 'Blue Hawaii' a cool-toned, almost metallic appearance in the garden that contrasts effectively with warm orange, yellow, or hot-pink tropical companions. The dark purple stems extend the ornamental interest — particularly visible when the large leaves move in a breeze, revealing the colorful stem as a secondary design element. Blue Hawaii is an outstanding tropical water garden and bog garden plant: like all Colocasia esculenta, it grows naturally in moist to seasonally flooded conditions and performs best in perpetually moist or wet soil, tolerating standing water at the margins of ponds and water features. In Gulf South zone 9, Blue Hawaii grows as a perennial with heavy winter mulching, returning vigorously each spring; in zone 10–11, it is evergreen. The tropical effect of an established clump at a water garden edge — combining the large blue-green leaves, dark stems, and reflected light from the water — is genuinely impressive and unique in the landscape.

common_name: Tifleaf Pearl Millet (High-Yield Summer Cover Crop and Wildlife Food Plot Grass)

planting.note: Direct sow 1/2 inch deep after the last frost when soil is consistently warm (65°F+); Tifleaf 3 can be broadcast-seeded or drilled at 15–25 lbs/acre for cover crop use; for garden plots, 1 oz per 100 square feet provides dense stand coverage; plant after spring crops are removed for a summer-to-fall cover crop terminated before fall vegetable plantings.

directions: Direct sow after the last frost when soil is warm in well-drained to moderately moist, average to fertile soil in full sun; Tifleaf Pearl Millet is the premier summer cover crop and wildlife food plot annual grass for Gulf South gardens and farms — a rapidly growing warm-season grass developed by the University of Georgia that establishes extremely quickly in warm conditions, producing dense, tall (5–8 foot) stands within 6–8 weeks of planting. The rapid establishment makes pearl millet exceptionally effective as a summer cover crop in Gulf South gardens that experience the challenging June–August gap between spring vegetable harvest and fall planting — a period when bare soil erodes, weeds proliferate, and soil organic matter degrades under summer sun. Tifleaf 3 is one of the most productive of all cover crop grasses for total biomass per acre in summer conditions, providing substantial green manure when terminated by mowing or incorporation before fall planting. The tall, dense stand also creates an effective summer screen, privacy hedge, or windbreak for garden areas. For wildlife gardeners, pearl millet provides outstanding deer forage and dove attractant — the seed heads produced in late summer are among the most palatable of all wildlife food plot plantings for mourning doves. Drought-tolerant once established. Terminated easily by mowing at the soil surface — the shallow root system decomposes quickly, improving soil organic matter. Grows as a warm-season annual, killed by first frost.
